title: FedFG: Privacy-Preserving and Robust Federated Learning via Flow-Matching Generation
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables distributed clients to collaboratively train a global model using local private data. Nevertheless, recent studies show that conventional FL algorithms still exhibit deficiencies in privacy protection, and the server lacks a reliable and stable aggregation rule for updating the global model. This situation creates opportunities for adversaries: on the one hand, they may eavesdrop on uploaded gradients or model parameters, potentially leaking benign clients' private data; on the other hand, they may compromise clients to launch poisoning attacks that corrupt the global model. To balance accuracy and security, we propose FedFG, a robust FL framework based on flow-matching generation that simultaneously preserves client privacy and resists sophisticated poisoning attacks. On the client side, each local network is decoupled into a private feature extractor and a public classifier. Each client is further equipped with a flow-matching generator that replaces the extractor when interacting with the server, thereby protecting private features while learning an approximation of the underlying data distribution. Complementing the client-side design, the server employs a client-update verification scheme and a novel robust aggregation mechanism driven by synthetic samples produced by the flow-matching generator. Experiments on MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that, compared with prior work, our approach adapts to multiple attack strategies and achieves higher accuracy while maintaining strong privacy protection.

sections.heading: IINTRODUCTION
sections.text:
Federated learning (FL) [ 3 , 26 ] has emerged as a key paradigm for collaborative model training across distributed clients, where raw data remain local and only model updates are exchanged. This design aligns with data protection regulations such as GDPR [ 29 ] and HIPAA [ 1 ] , and has enabled deployments in security-critical domains, including healthcare, autonomous driving, and financial services [ 30 , 27 , 8 , 23 , 10 ] . However, as FL is increasingly used to support high-stakes decision making, its security assumptions are being challenged by adversaries operating over open networks and in partially compromised client populations.

A central challenge is that FL must simultaneously ensure data confidentiality and model integrity.
On the one hand, adversaries can reconstruct high-fidelity private images or text from observed client gradients via privacy attacks, leading to sensitive information leakage [ 47 , 45 , 12 ] ; on the other hand, adversaries can control malicious clients to launch advanced poisoning attacks [ 15 , 39 , 11 , 5 , 40 ] , thereby degrading global model performance and undermining system security. These two risks are tightly coupled: mechanisms that expose more information for server-side inspection may improve robustness but worsen privacy, whereas mechanisms that conceal updates can strengthen privacy but reduce the server’s ability to detect malicious contributions.

Existing defenses often address only one side of this coupled problem or rely on assumptions that break down in practical non-IID settings. Cryptographic protocols can prevent plaintext exposure [ 14 , 21 , 9 ] , but their overhead can be prohibitive; more fundamentally, encrypted updates limit the server’s ability to verify client behavior [ 32 ] . Differential privacy (DP) provides formal protection by perturbing updates [ 35 , 41 , 36 ] , yet stringent privacy budgets can substantially degrade accuracy. Byzantine-robust aggregators such as Krum [ 2 ] and the coordinate-wise median [ 43 ] rely on geometric clustering of benign updates, an assumption often violated under heterogeneous client distributions, and can be bypassed by sophisticated attacks [ 39 ] . Validation-based schemes can improve robustness by screening updates [ 4 , 46 ] , but typically require auxiliary clean data or trusted validation signals, which may conflict with the “no extra data” premise and introduce additional privacy concerns.

Motivated by this tension, we explore a different design axis: using synthetic data as a privacy–robustness bridge. Unlike raw updates, synthetic samples can be kept in plaintext to enable server-side verification, while avoiding a one-to-one correspondence with original instances, thereby reducing direct leakage risks. This perspective suggests a unified architecture in which clients provide behavioral evidence via generated samples rather than revealing sensitive feature representations or full parameter updates. As a result, the server can assess update quality and detect poisoning attempts without directly inspecting private client updates.

Building on this insight, we propose FedFG, a robust federated learning framework based on flow-matching generation that jointly preserves privacy and resists sophisticated poisoning attacks. On the client side, each model is decoupled into a private extractor and a public classifier, and a flow-matching generator is trained to replace the extractor when interacting with the server, protecting private features while learning an approximation to the local data distribution. On the server side, we design a client update verification scheme and a robust aggregation mechanism driven by synthetic samples produced by these generators, enabling anomaly detection and accuracy-aware reweighting under diverse attack strategies. The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:

• We propose FedFG, a federated learning framework that leverages flow-matching generators to unify client-side privacy protection and server-side robustness within a single design. • We design a server-side verification and aggregation scheme that uses synthetic samples to compute outlier and accuracy scores, detect malicious clients, and perform accuracy-aware reweighting over the remaining clients. • We establish a first-order stationarity guarantee for FedFG under nonconvex objectives, providing a rigorous theoretical foundation for federated learning framework that simultaneously address privacy preservation and model poisoning defense. • To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to adopt flow-matching as the generative backbone for a unified federated learning architecture capable of mitigating both privacy leakage and poisoning attacks. Extensive experiments and thorough evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our approach.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In section ˜ II , we review related work and introduce the flow-matching model. In section ˜ III , we present the proposed framework, which uses flow-matching generators as a cornerstone for client-side privacy protection and server-side robustness, and analyze its convergence in section ˜ IV . In section ˜ V , we detail the experimental settings and report results from comparative evaluations. Finally, in section ˜ VI , we conclude and discuss potential directions for future work.

sections.heading: IIRELATED WORK
sections.text: In this section, we review prior work on privacy-preserving and robust federated learning, covering major security threats and corresponding defense strategies. We also discuss generative federated learning and flow-matching models, the latter of which provides the foundation for our method.

sections.heading: II-APrivacy Leakage Protection
sections.text: Despite keeping raw data local, FL remains vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks [ 47 , 45 , 12 ] that reconstruct training samples from shared updates. Consequently, explicit protection mechanisms are required, typically involving trade-offs among accuracy, robustness, and communication overhead. To mitigate privacy leakage caused by directly uploading local updates, existing defenses are commonly categorized into three groups: cryptography-based methods, differential privacy-based methods, and model-masking methods.

sections.heading: II-BModel Poisoning Defense
sections.text: In model poisoning attacks, adversaries manipulate local updates to degrade global model performance. Representative strategies include sign flipping [ 15 ] , inner product manipulation [ 39 ] , and optimization-based attacks [ 11 , 5 , 40 ] that maximize deviation or steer the model toward attacker-chosen low-accuracy states over multiple rounds. To defend against model poisoning in federated learning, existing studies can be broadly grouped into three lines: statistically robust aggregation, similarity-based detection, and validation-based filtering.

sections.heading: II-CGenerative Federated Learning and Flow-Matching Models
sections.text:
Existing generative federated learning (GFL) methods often address privacy or robustness in isolation rather than jointly. Client-side generation approaches [ 37 , 25 ] use generators to replace privacy-sensitive representations, but typically lack robust server-side verification mechanisms. Server-side generation approaches [ 46 , 44 ] produce audit samples for scoring client updates, but may depend on auxiliary clean validation data and can be unstable under non-IID heterogeneity. These limitations motivate a unified architecture in which the generative component supports both privacy protection and robust aggregation.

Recent flow-matching methods provide a promising foundation by enabling simulation-free training of continuous normalizing flows (CNFs) [ 22 , 33 ] . A CNF defines a deterministic probability-flow ODE d ​ x t d ​ t = v θ ​ ( t , x t ) , \frac{dx_{t}}{dt}=v_{\theta}(t,x_{t}), (1) which transports samples from a source distribution to the data distribution. Flow matching trains v θ v_{\theta} via regression to a target vector field: ℒ FM ​ ( θ ) = 𝔼 t , x ∼ p t ​ ‖ v θ ​ ( t , x ) − u t ​ ( x ) ‖ 2 2 . \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{FM}}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{t,x\sim p_{t}}\big\|v_{\theta}(t,x)-u_{t}(x)\big\|_{2}^{2}. (2) Importantly, optimal-transport displacement interpolation can yield straighter conditional trajectories, enabling faster and more stable generation with fewer neural function evaluations [ 22 , 33 , 24 ] . These properties make flow matching well suited to FL: it supports stable sample synthesis under non-IID settings and produces plaintext synthetic data that the server can use for update verification and robust aggregation. Accordingly, we adopt flow matching as the generative backbone and propose FedFG, which tightly couples client-side privacy protection with server-side robustness via flow-matching generated synthetic samples.

sections.heading: IIIPROPOSED METHOD
sections.text:
In this section, we present FedFG, a secure and privacy-preserving federated learning framework based on flow-matching generation. As illustrated in Figure 1 , the framework consists of two iterative procedures.

We consider a standard cross-silo FL system consisting of a central server and N N clients. Client i i holds a private dataset 𝒟 i = { ( x , y ) } \mathcal{D}_{i}=\{(x,y)\} , where x x and y ∈ { 1 , … , K } y\in\{1,\dots,K\} denote an input sample and its label, respectively.
FedFG decouples each client model into a private feature extractor E i : 𝒳 → ℝ d E_{i}:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{d} and a public classifier C i : ℝ d → ℝ K C_{i}:\mathbb{R}^{d}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{K} , and further equips each client with a flow-matching generator F ​ G i : 𝒵 × { 1 , … , K } → ℝ d FG_{i}:\mathcal{Z}\times\{1,\dots,K\}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{d} that approximates the feature distribution induced by the extractor, where 𝒵 \mathcal{Z} is a simple noise distribution (e.g., a standard Gaussian). The server maintains a global generator F ​ G g FG_{g} and a global classifier C g C_{g} . In each communication round r r , clients download ( θ F ​ G g r − 1 , θ C g r − 1 ) (\theta_{FG_{g}}^{r-1},\theta_{C_{g}}^{r-1}) , perform local updates, and upload ( θ F ​ G i r , θ C i r ) (\theta_{FG_{i}}^{r},\theta_{C_{i}}^{r}) . The server then verifies client updates using synthetic feature probes produced by F ​ G g FG_{g} and performs robust aggregation to obtain ( θ F ​ G g r , θ C g r ) (\theta_{FG_{g}}^{r},\theta_{C_{g}}^{r}) . Details are provided below for both the client-side and server-side procedures.

sections.heading: III-AClient Side: Privacy-Preserving Local Update
sections.text:
A key privacy risk in FL stems from sharing parameters that directly interact with raw data, enabling gradient inversion and related reconstruction attacks. FedFG mitigates this risk by keeping the extractor E i E_{i} strictly local. Only ( θ F ​ G i , θ C i ) (\theta_{FG_{i}},\theta_{C_{i}}) are exchanged with the server. Since F ​ G i FG_{i} operates in feature space and does not process raw inputs, and since C i C_{i} maps features to logits, the information directly exposed about raw samples is substantially reduced compared with sharing an end-to-end model. Algorithm 1 describes the procedure in detail.

At round r r , client i i initializes its classifier from the global classifier and then updates θ E i \theta_{E_{i}} and θ C i \theta_{C_{i}} on its private data by minimizing the supervised classification loss: ℒ cls ​ ( θ E i , θ C i ) = 𝔼 ( x , y ) ∼ 𝒟 i ​ [ Ω ​ ( C i ​ ( E i ​ ( x ; θ E i ) ; θ C i ) , y ) ] , \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{cls}}(\theta_{E_{i}},\theta_{C_{i}})=\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{i}}\Big[\Omega\big(C_{i}(E_{i}(x;\theta_{E_{i}});\theta_{C_{i}}),\,y\big)\Big], (3) where Ω ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) \Omega(\cdot,\cdot) denotes the cross-entropy loss. The local update is: ( θ E i , θ C i ) ← ( θ E i , θ C i ) − η 1 ​ ∇ ℒ cls , (\theta_{E_{i}},\theta_{C_{i}})\leftarrow(\theta_{E_{i}},\theta_{C_{i}})-\eta_{1}\nabla\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{cls}}, (4) with learning rate η 1 \eta_{1} .

After updating the extractor, client i i trains a flow-matching generator F ​ G i FG_{i} to approximate the conditional feature distribution of the extractor outputs given label y y .
Let h = E i ​ ( x ; θ E i ) h=E_{i}(x;\theta_{E_{i}}) (5) denote the real feature. Client i i optimizes θ F ​ G i \theta_{FG_{i}} via a flow-matching objective: ℒ FM ​ ( θ F ​ G i ; θ E i ) = 𝔼 ( x , y ) ∼ 𝒟 i , z ∼ p ​ ( z ) ​ [ ℓ FM ​ ( F ​ G i , E i ​ ( x ) , y , z ) ] , \!\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{FM}}\!\big(\!\theta_{FG_{i}};\!\theta_{E_{i}}\!\big)\!=\!\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{D}_{i},z\sim p(z)}\!\Big[\ell_{\mathrm{FM}}\!\big(\!FG_{i},\!E_{i}(x),\!y,\!z\!\big)\!\Big], (6) where ℓ FM ​ ( ⋅ ) \ell_{\mathrm{FM}}(\cdot) is the per-sample flow-matching loss implemented by the client-side generator module.
The generator update is: θ F ​ G i ← θ F ​ G i − η 2 ​ ∇ ℒ FM , \theta_{FG_{i}}\leftarrow\theta_{FG_{i}}-\eta_{2}\nabla\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{FM}}, (7) with learning rate η 2 \eta_{2} .

To instantiate a standard flow-matching model, we parameterize the generator as a conditional, time-dependent vector field v θ F ​ G i ​ ( h , t , y ) v_{\theta_{FG_{i}}}(h,t,y) and define generation through the ordinary differential equation d ​ h ​ ( t ) d ​ t = v θ F ​ G i ​ ( h ​ ( t ) , t , y ) , t ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] , \frac{dh(t)}{dt}=v_{\theta_{FG_{i}}}(h(t),t,y),\quad t\in[0,1], (8) where the initial state h ​ ( 0 ) h(0) is sampled from a simple prior induced by z ∼ p ​ ( z ) z\sim p(z) and a lightweight mapping from noise space to feature space. Sampling corresponds to integrating ( 8 ) from t = 0 t=0 to t = 1 t=1 to obtain h ​ ( 1 ) h(1) .

For training, we adopt an independent conditional flow-matching probability path between a noise sample h 0 h_{0} and a real feature h 1 = h h_{1}=h as mentioned in Eq. ( 5 ): h t = t ​ h 1 + ( 1 − t ) ​ h 0 + σ ​ ϵ , \displaystyle h_{t}=th_{1}+(1-t)h_{0}+\sigma\epsilon, (9) h 0 ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , I ) , ϵ ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , I ) , t ∼ Unif ​ ( 0 , 1 ) , \displaystyle h_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),\ \epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I),\ t\sim\mathrm{Unif}(0,1), (10) where σ ≥ 0 \sigma\geq 0 is a scalar that controls the stochasticity of the path.
Under this construction, the conditional target flow is u t ​ ( h 1 ∣ h 0 ) = h 1 − h 0 , u_{t}(h_{1}\mid h_{0})=h_{1}-h_{0}, (11) and the per-sample flow-matching loss can be written as the mean-squared error between the predicted and target flows: ℓ FM ( F G i , h 1 , y , z ) = 𝔼 t [ ∥ v θ F ​ G i ( h t , t , y ) − u t ( h 1 ∣ h 0 ) ∥ 2 2 ] . \ell_{\mathrm{FM}}\!\big(FG_{i},\!h_{1},\!y,\!z\big)\!=\!\mathbb{E}_{t}\!\Big[\big\|v_{\theta_{FG_{i}}}(h_{t},\!t,\!y)-u_{t}(h_{1}\!\mid\!h_{0})\big\|_{2}^{2}\Big]. (12) The condition y y is injected into v θ F ​ G i v_{\theta_{FG_{i}}} via a learnable label embedding, and time t t is provided through a suitable time embedding, enabling the model to learn class-specific transport dynamics in the extractor feature space. During this stage, θ E i \theta_{E_{i}} is fixed and only θ F ​ G i \theta_{FG_{i}} is updated, so the generator learns the conditional feature distribution induced by the current extractor.

After the two-stage update, client i i uploads only the public modules ( θ F ​ G i r , θ C i r ) (\theta_{FG_{i}}^{r},\,\theta_{C_{i}}^{r}) , while keeping θ E i r \theta_{E_{i}}^{r} local. This design preserves privacy by preventing the server from accessing the module that directly transforms raw inputs into intermediate representations.

sections.heading: III-BServer Side: Update Verification and Robust Aggregation
sections.text:
As illustrated in Figure 1 , malicious clients ( i ∈ ℳ i\in\mathcal{M} ) may apply a poisoning function 𝒜 ​ ( ⋅ ) \mathcal{A}(\cdot) to their public parameters before uploading (e.g., inner product manipulation), aiming to degrade the global model accuracy through corrupted updates. To counter such threats, FedFG verifies client updates using synthetic feature probes generated by the aggregated generator and filters outliers prior to aggregation. The key idea is to let F ​ G g FG_{g} act as a judge that produces class-conditional synthetic features, which are then fed into each client classifier to obtain comparable predictive distributions for distinguishing benign updates from poisoned ones.

The server first forms preliminary global models by weighted averaging: θ F ​ G g r ← ∑ i = 1 N w i ( r − 1 ) ​ θ F ​ G i r , θ C g r ← ∑ i = 1 N w i ( r − 1 ) ​ θ C i r , \theta_{FG_{g}}^{r}\leftarrow\sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(r-1)}\,\theta_{FG_{i}}^{r},\quad\theta_{C_{g}}^{r}\leftarrow\sum_{i=1}^{N}w_{i}^{(r-1)}\,\theta_{C_{i}}^{r}, (13) where w i ( r − 1 ) w_{i}^{(r-1)} is the server’s current aggregation weight for client i i (initialized proportionally to local data sizes and later updated using accuracy scores; see below). This preliminary F ​ G g r FG_{g}^{r} is then used to generate synthetic feature probes for verification.

The server samples labels y ∼ Unif ​ ( { 1 , … , K } ) y\sim\mathrm{Unif}(\{1,\dots,K\}) and noise z ∼ p ​ ( z ) z\sim p(z) , and generates synthetic features h ~ = F ​ G g r ​ ( z , y ; θ F ​ G g r ) . \tilde{h}=FG_{g}^{r}(z,y;\theta_{FG_{g}}^{r}). (14) For each client classifier C i r C_{i}^{r} , the server evaluates its correctness on these synthetic pairs: s i r = 𝔼 y , z ​ [ 𝕀 ​ ( arg ⁡ max ⁡ C i r ​ ( h ~ ) = y ) ] , s_{i}^{r}=\mathbb{E}_{y,z}\Big[\mathbb{I}\big(\arg\max C_{i}^{r}(\tilde{h})=y\big)\Big], (15) where 𝕀 ​ ( ⋅ ) \mathbb{I}(\cdot) is the indicator function and the expectation is approximated via Monte Carlo batches.
The scores are normalized to obtain the relative accuracy score: α i r = s i r ∑ j = 1 N s j r + ε . \alpha_{i}^{r}=\frac{s_{i}^{r}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N}s_{j}^{r}+\varepsilon}. (16)

Using the same synthetic probes ( h ~ , y ) (\tilde{h},y) , the server computes the predictive probability vector of client i i : p i ​ ( h ~ ) = softmax ​ ( C i r ​ ( h ~ ) ) ∈ Δ K − 1 . p_{i}(\tilde{h})=\mathrm{softmax}\big(C_{i}^{r}(\tilde{h})\big)\in\Delta^{K-1}. (17) For two clients i i and j j , their predictive discrepancy is measured by the Hellinger distance: d H ​ ( p i , p j ) = 1 2 ​ ‖ p i − p j ‖ 2 . d_{\mathrm{H}}\big(p_{i},p_{j}\big)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left\|\sqrt{p_{i}}-\sqrt{p_{j}}\right\|_{2}. (18) We define the outlier score of client i i as the average pairwise distance to all other clients: o i r = 1 N − 1 ​ ∑ j ≠ i 𝔼 y , z ​ [ d H ​ ( p i ​ ( h ~ ) , p j ​ ( h ~ ) ) ] . o_{i}^{r}=\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{j\neq i}\mathbb{E}_{y,z}\Big[d_{\mathrm{H}}\big(p_{i}(\tilde{h}),p_{j}(\tilde{h})\big)\Big]. (19) Intuitively, poisoning often yields classifiers whose predictive behavior on shared probes deviates from that of benign clients, leading to larger o i r o_{i}^{r} .

To robustly identify outliers without assuming a parametric distribution, the server applies a Hampel rule based on the median and MAD: m r \displaystyle m^{r} = median ​ { o i r } i = 1 N , \displaystyle=\mathrm{median}\{o_{i}^{r}\}_{i=1}^{N}, (20) MAD r \displaystyle\mathrm{MAD}^{r} = median ​ { | o i r − m r | } i = 1 N + ε , \displaystyle=\mathrm{median}\{|o_{i}^{r}-m^{r}|\}_{i=1}^{N}+\varepsilon, (21) τ r \displaystyle\tau^{r} = m r + γ ⋅ 1.4826 ⋅ MAD r . \displaystyle=m^{r}+\gamma\cdot 1.4826\cdot\mathrm{MAD}^{r}. (22) Here, γ \gamma is a tunable cutoff in units of the MAD-based scale, and 1.4826 1.4826 rescales MAD \mathrm{MAD} to be σ \sigma -equivalent under Gaussian noise. Client i i is flagged as an outlier if o i r > τ r o_{i}^{r}>\tau^{r} .

In addition to the outlier test, FedFG filters low-accuracy updates by thresholding the relative accuracy scores: client i i is filtered if α i r < κ \alpha_{i}^{r}<\kappa . The two filters are combined by set union.

Let ℬ r \mathcal{B}^{r} denote the set of remaining benign clients after filtering. The server re-normalizes the accuracy weights over ℬ r \mathcal{B}^{r} : α ¯ i r = α i r ∑ j ∈ ℬ r α j r + ε , i ∈ ℬ r . \bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}=\frac{\alpha_{i}^{r}}{\sum_{j\in\mathcal{B}^{r}}\alpha_{j}^{r}+\varepsilon},\qquad i\in\mathcal{B}^{r}. (23) Finally, the server aggregates only benign updates: θ F ​ G g r ← ∑ i ∈ ℬ r α ¯ i r ​ θ F ​ G i r , θ C g r ← ∑ i ∈ ℬ r α ¯ i r ​ θ C i r . \theta_{FG_{g}}^{r}\leftarrow\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^{r}}\bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}\,\theta_{FG_{i}}^{r},\quad\theta_{C_{g}}^{r}\leftarrow\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}^{r}}\bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}\,\theta_{C_{i}}^{r}. (24) The resulting ( θ F ​ G g r , θ C g r ) (\theta_{FG_{g}}^{r},\theta_{C_{g}}^{r}) are broadcast to all clients for the next round. Moreover, we set the next-round prior weights as w i ( r ) = α i r w_{i}^{(r)}=\alpha_{i}^{r} , yielding an adaptive, history-aware aggregation scheme. The full procedure is presented in Algorithm 2 .

sections.heading: IVConvergence Analysis
sections.text:
We establish a first-order stationarity guarantee for FedFG. Let ℬ \mathcal{B} and ℳ \mathcal{M} partition the N N clients into benign and malicious sets. We stack the server-maintained public parameters as θ r := [ θ C g r ; θ F ​ G g r ] ∈ ℝ p \theta^{r}:=[\theta_{C_{g}}^{r};\,\theta_{FG_{g}}^{r}]\in\mathbb{R}^{p} and define each benign client’s local objective as F i ​ ( θ ) := 𝔼 ξ ∼ 𝒟 i ​ [ ℓ ​ ( θ ; ξ ) ] F_{i}(\theta):=\mathbb{E}_{\xi\sim\mathcal{D}_{i}}[\ell(\theta;\xi)] for i ∈ ℬ i\in\mathcal{B} . The accuracy-weighted global objective at round r r is F ~ r ​ ( θ ) := ∑ i ∈ ℬ α ¯ i r ​ F i ​ ( θ ) , \widetilde{F}_{r}(\theta):=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}}\bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}\,F_{i}(\theta), (25) where α ¯ i r ≥ 0 \bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}\geq 0 , ∑ i ∈ ℬ α ¯ i r = 1 \sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}}\bar{\alpha}_{i}^{r}=1 are the renormalized accuracy-aware weights from Eq. ( 23 ). Let F ∗ , r ​ ( θ ) := ∑ i ∈ ℬ α ¯ i , ∗ r ​ F i ​ ( θ ) F_{*,r}(\theta):=\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}}\bar{\alpha}_{i,*}^{r}\,F_{i}(\theta) denote the population-weight counterpart obtained with infinitely many synthetic probes, and let ℰ r := { ℬ r = ℬ } \mathcal{E}^{r}:=\{\mathcal{B}^{r}=\mathcal{B}\} be the event that the server correctly identifies all benign clients at round r r .

We introduce the following assumptions required for the analysis. Assumptions IV.1 – IV.4 are standard in federated optimization [ 20 , 16 ] , while Assumptions IV.5 – IV.6 capture the specific properties of FedFG’s verification mechanism.

Based on these assumptions, we present two supporting lemmas.

We now state the main convergence result.

sections.heading: VEXPERIMENTAL ANALYSIS
sections.text: In this section, we evaluate the performance of FedFG in terms of privacy protection and robustness to data poisoning on three representative federated learning datasets.

sections.heading: V-BEvaluation on Privacy Protection Ability
sections.text: FedFG provides privacy protection by updating the flow-matching generator F ​ G i t FG_{i}^{t} rather than directly updating the private extractor E i t E_{i}^{t} . To evaluate its effectiveness, we conduct experiments against gradient-based privacy attacks as follows.

sections.heading: VICONCLUSION
sections.text: In this paper, we propose FedFG, a robust federated learning framework based on flow-matching generation that jointly preserves client privacy and defends against sophisticated poisoning attacks. FedFG decouples each client model into a private extractor and a public classifier, and uses a flow-matching generator to replace the extractor during server communication, thereby reducing privacy leakage from shared updates. On the server side, FedFG verifies client updates using synthetic feature probes and performs robust aggregation via outlier filtering and accuracy-aware reweighting. Extensive experiments under both IID and non-IID settings show that FedFG consistently outperforms state-of-the-art defenses under multiple Byzantine attack strategies. Despite these promising results, FedFG introduces additional computation and communication overhead due to generator training and probe-based verification, which may be challenging for large-scale client populations. Future work will focus on improving efficiency for large-scale deployments and extending the framework to more adaptive threats and more complex real-world scenarios.

figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of FedFG. On each client, the local model is decoupled into a private feature extractor and a public classifier, and is equipped with a flow-matching generator that replaces the extractor during communication with the server. The server collects model parameters from clients and performs robust aggregation with three modules: 1) evaluation of outlier and accuracy scores via synthetic samples generated by the flow-matching generator; 2) detection of malicious clients based on the Hampel rule and accuracy threshold; and 3) accuracy-aware reweighting aggregation over the remaining benign clients.

Figure 2: Comparison of ground truth images and recovered images under DLG and IG attacks. The PSNR (in dB) and SSIM values are listed sequentially below each image. The first row reports results on MNIST, the second row reports results on FMNIST, and the third and fourth rows report results on CIFAR10.
Figure 6: Accuracy comparison of Median, DPR-PPFL, GAN-Filter, and FedFG on MNIST and MNIST-0.5 under SF and IPM when the attack starts at round 0.
